questions.question: What is the key difference in how a latch and a flip-flop respond to a clock signal?
options: A latch is level-sensitive, while a flip-flop is edge-triggered.
A latch stores multiple bits, while a flip-flop stores only one.
A latch has an asynchronous reset, while a flip-flop has a synchronous reset.
A latch uses a D input, while a flip-flop uses a JK input.
A latch is used for combinational logic, while a flip-flop is used for sequential logic.

questions.question: In a pipelined processor, which type of hazard occurs when an instruction attempts to read a register that has not yet been written by a preceding instruction in the pipeline?
options: Structural Hazard
Control Hazard
Data Hazard (RAW - Read After Write)
Branch Hazard
Resource Hazard

questions.question: In the physical design of an Integrated Circuit (IC), what is the primary purpose of designing a robust "power delivery network" (PDN) or "power grid"?
options: To minimize the total gate count of the design.
To ensure all clock signals arrive simultaneously at all sequential elements.
To provide stable and sufficient power (VDD and VSS) to all transistors across the chip, minimizing voltage drops (IR drop) and noise.
To optimize the placement of standard cells for minimal wire length.
To identify and fix all design rule checking (DRC) violations.

questions.question: In advanced multi-core System-on-Chip (SoC) designs, what is the primary motivation for employing a Network-on-Chip (NoC) architecture instead of traditional bus-based interconnects?
options: To simplify the design verification process by reducing the number of interfaces.
To achieve lower power consumption by reducing overall switching activity.
To provide higher scalability, bandwidth, and lower latency communication for a large number of cores and intellectual property (IP) blocks.
To enable faster gate-level simulation for complex designs.
To eliminate the need for memory management units (MMUs) in each core.

questions.question: In Verilog HDL, what is the fundamental difference between a `wire` data type and a `reg` data type?
options: `wire` is used for storing combinational logic outputs, while `reg` is used for sequential logic outputs only.
`wire` represents physical connections and cannot store a value, while `reg` can store a value and is used for procedural assignments.
`wire` can only hold 0 or 1, while `reg` can hold X or Z states.
`wire` is synthesizable, but `reg` is only used for simulation.
`wire` is a 1-bit signal, while `reg` can be multi-bit.
